conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. 

Before I can assess your eligibility, I need to gather some information about your circumstances. Let me first review the official eligibility rules.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for your patience. Now I can help you determine your eligibility for Child Benefit.

To get started, could you please tell me: **Do you have any children you're responsible for?** If yes, how many children are you asking about?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hi! Yes, I'm asking about my child Alex. He's 16 years old and lives with me here in Manchester.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! So you're asking about Alex, who is 16 years old and lives with you in Manchester.

Since Alex is 16, I need to ask: **Is Alex currently in approved education or training?** (For example, studying at school, college, or in an approved training program)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Well, he's not in education or training right now. He left school recently, but he has signed up with the careers service to figure out what he wants to do next.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing that information. That's very helpful to know.

Just to clarify: **When did Alex leave education, and approximately when did he register with the careers service?** (I'm asking because there are specific time periods that apply in this situation)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: It's been quite recent - he left education and registered with the careers service within the last few months. Why do you ask?

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that clarification. I'm asking because there's a specific rule that applies to 16 and 17 year olds who leave education or training.

According to the Child Benefit rules, you can apply for Child Benefit to continue for **20 weeks** if a 16 or 17 year old leaves education or training and registers with a government-sponsored careers service (or the armed services).

To give you an accurate assessment, I need to know: **Has it been more than 20 weeks since Alex left education and registered with the careers service, or is it still within that 20-week period?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Oh, it's still within that 20-week period. He only left and registered a couple of months ago.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for confirming that! That's helpful information.

Just a few more questions to complete my assessment:

1. **Are you responsible for Alex** - meaning you live with him (which you've confirmed) and provide for his needs?
2. **Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex?**
3. **Do you live in the UK?** (You mentioned Manchester, but I just need to confirm)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I'm responsible for Alex - he lives at home with me and I provide for all his needs. No one else is claiming Child Benefit for him. And yes, I live in the UK, in Manchester.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect, thank you for providing all that information. Let me just confirm one final detail:

**Has Alex started any paid work for 24 hours or more per week, or is he claiming any benefits in his own right** (such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he hasn't started any paid work like that, and he's not claiming any benefits in his own right.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent news! **You are eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.**

Here's why: Since Alex is 16 and has left education but registered with the careers service within the last couple of months, you can continue to receive Child Benefit for up to **20 weeks** from when he left education and registered. You meet all the eligibility criteria:

- You're responsible for Alex and he lives with you
- You live in the UK
- No one else is claiming Child Benefit for him
- Alex hasn't started paid work for 24+ hours per week
- Alex isn't claiming benefits in his own right
- He's still within the 20-week period after leaving education and registering with the careers service

**Important to note:** This eligibility will continue for the 20-week period. After that, if Alex is still not in approved education or training, your Child Benefit for him would stop unless he enters approved education, training, or an apprenticeship.

If you haven't already claimed Child Benefit, you should apply. If you're already receiving it, it should continue during this 20-week period.

Is there anything else you'd like to know about Child Benefit?

claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 16 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 16 and in the 20-week extension period
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 16 and in the 20-week extension period; Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is 16 and in the 20-week extension period
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

reasonings: Alex is 16 years old and has left education. According to the Child Benefit eligibility rules, you can apply for Child Benefit to continue for 20 weeks if a 16 or 17 year old leaves education or training and registers with a government-sponsored careers service. Alex left education and registered with the careers service within the last couple of months, which is still within the 20-week period. The claimant is responsible for Alex (lives with him and provides for his needs), lives in the UK (Manchester), no one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex, and Alex has not started paid work for 24 hours or more per week nor is he claiming benefits in his own right. Therefore, the claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for Alex during this 20-week period.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant is responsible for one child, Alex (16 years old), who lives with them in Manchester, UK. Alex recently left education and registered with the careers service within the last few months (still within the 20-week eligibility period). No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex, and Alex is not working 24+ hours per week or claiming benefits in his own right. The claimant meets all eligibility criteria for Child Benefit for Alex under the special provision for 16-17 year olds who have left education and registered with a careers service.
